cards.definition: A farming approach built around no tillage, no external fertilizer, cow-based bioformulations, continuous mulching, and on-farm compost production — aiming to cover a farm's full nutrient need without bought chemical inputs.
cards.analogy: Running a household entirely on what's grown and made on-site, instead of buying groceries for meals you could have produced yourself.
cards.why_it_matters: A documented reference point for input-cost reduction — worth asking about directly if a farm mentions unusually low fertilizer spend.
cards.cloze: ___ is a farming approach built on no tillage, no external fertilizer, cow-based inputs, and on-farm compost to cover nutrient needs.

cards.definition: A pair of cow-based bioformulations central to ZBNF, applied to the field roughly three times a year at scale, in place of external chemical fertilizer.
cards.analogy: A homemade starter culture used to keep an entire system fermenting and alive, reapplied on a regular schedule.
cards.why_it_matters: Specific enough to ask about by name — a farmer using these can point to exactly what they're applying and how often.
cards.cloze: ___ are the cow-based bioformulations central to ZBNF, applied to the field roughly three times a year.

cards.definition: Compost produced on-site from farm waste like leaf litter, pulp, and pruning residue, at a scale capable of covering a farm's entire nutrient requirement without bought fertilizer.
cards.analogy: Cooking from what's already in the fridge — labor-intensive to set up, but once running it covers the whole need at near-zero marginal cost.
cards.why_it_matters: The nutrient-source half of the ZBNF cost story — worth asking whether a farm's vermicompost covers full need or just supplements bought fertilizer.
cards.cloze: ___ is compost produced on-site from farm waste, at a scale capable of covering a farm's entire nutrient requirement.
